original_hypothesis.title: rebalanceSwap Optimistic State Update + Unchecked transferFrom Return Value Enables Free Token Extraction
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: rebalanceSwap updates reserves (reserveValidatorToken += amountIn, reserveUserToken -= amountOut) BEFORE calling IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom. The return value of transferFrom is not checked (no SafeERC20). If validatorToken returns false instead of reverting on failure, the state updates persist but no tokens are received. The subsequent IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) sends real userTokens to the attacker for free. The attacker simply calls rebalanceSwap with zero allowance; transferFrom returns false; they receive userTokens without paying.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is uniquely dangerous because the state-before-transfer pattern in rebalanceSwap means ANY token that returns false on transferFrom failure (rather than reverting) creates a direct extraction vulnerability. The contract uses bare IERC20 interface without SafeERC20, so return values are discarded.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) and pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) at ~lines 148-149; IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom at ~line 151 (return value unchecked); IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) at ~line 155

preconditions: validatorToken returns false (not reverts) on failed transferFrom
Attacker has zero allowance or zero balance for validatorToken

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State updates at lines ~148-149 before transferFrom at ~151. IERC20 interface returns bool but code does not check it. Discarded return value enables silent failures.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No SafeERC20 library used anywhere in contract
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — any call to rebalanceSwap with a non-reverting-on-fail validatorToken

operational_questions: Which TIP-20 stablecoin implementations return false vs revert on transfer failure?
Are there any pools where validatorToken has non-reverting failure behavior?
Can a non-reverting token be introduced via permissionless pool creation?
validation_requirements: Test with ERC20 that returns false on insufficient allowance
Verify that rebalanceSwap proceeds to transfer userTokens after silent transferFrom failure
Quantify maximum extractable amount per call

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or create pool where validatorToken returns false on transferFrom failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call rebalanceSwap with zero allowance for validatorToken and desired amountOut
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: transferFrom returns false silently; state already updated; userToken transferred to attacker

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete drain of userToken reserves from affected pool
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Pool becomes insolvent; reserves show phantom validatorToken balances
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All LPs and swappers in the affected pool

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low — no custom contract needed, just call with zero allowance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: RebalanceSwap events where sender has zero allowance for validatorToken
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: reserveValidatorToken growing without corresponding balance increase

validation_result.mechanism: Unchecked transferFrom return value + optimistic state update
validation_result.impact_summary: If validatorToken returns false on failed transferFrom (instead of reverting), an attacker can extract userTokens from any pool without providing validatorTokens, draining all userToken reserves.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The rebalanceSwap function in FeeAMM.sol contains two compounding defects: (1) reserves are updated optimistically before any token transfers occur, and (2) the return value of IERC20.transferFrom is not checked. In Solidity 0.8.x, calling IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom(...) through the interface performs ABI decoding of the return data. If the token returns abi.encode(false), the decoding succeeds (valid bool encoding), but since the return value is not captured or checked, execution silently continues. The state has already been updated (reserveValidatorToken increased, reserveUserToken decreased), so the subsequent IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) sends real tokens to the attacker. This is exactly the class of bug that OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library was designed to prevent. The contract imports IERC20 but never uses SafeERC20 anywhere.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines in rebalanceSwap clearly show: (a) state updates at lines containing pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) and pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) occur BEFORE (b) IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amountIn) which has no return value check, followed by (c) IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) which sends real tokens. No SafeERC20 import or usage exists anywhere in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: State is updated optimistically before transferFrom. The transferFrom return value (bool) is discarded. If validatorToken returns false on failure (insufficient allowance/balance), no tokens are received but reserveUserToken has already been decremented and IERC20(userToken).transfer sends real tokens to the attacker. This enables free token extraction.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls rebalanceSwap(userToken, validatorToken, amountOut, attackerAddress) with zero allowance for validatorToken
3. Checks pass: pool has sufficient reserveUserToken >= amountOut
4. pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) — state updated, phantom tokens credited
5. pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) — state updated, reserves decremented
6. IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom(attacker, this, amountIn) — returns false due to zero allowance; return value discarded
7. IERC20(userToken).transfer(attackerAddress, amountOut) — real tokens sent to attacker
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in the ordering of operations and the missing return value check. The contract uses the IERC20 interface (which declares transferFrom as returning bool), but the return value is not assigned to a variable, not passed to require(), and no SafeERC20 wrapper is used. In Solidity 0.8.x, calling a function through an interface that returns bool will ABI-decode the returned data. If the token returns abi.encode(false), the decoding succeeds (it's valid ABI for a bool), but since the code discards the result, execution continues to line IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) which sends real tokens. The optimistic state update at the prior lines means the pool accounting is already corrupted.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that would affect this vulnerability. The foundry.toml confirms solc 0.8.28 which does NOT auto-revert on false returns from interface calls — it only reverts on missing/malformed return data.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward: (1) Identify or create a pool where validatorToken is an ERC20 that returns false on transferFrom failure instead of reverting (e.g., ZRX, or a custom token). Pool creation is permissionless via mint(). (2) Ensure the pool has non-zero reserveUserToken (accumulated from executeFeeSwap calls). (3) Call rebalanceSwap with the desired amountOut and zero allowance for validatorToken. (4) transferFrom returns false silently. (5) userTokens are transferred to the attacker. (6) Repeat until reserveUserToken is fully drained. The attack requires no special contracts, governance tokens, or timing — just a single permissionless function call per iteration. Note: even if no existing pool has a false-returning validatorToken, an attacker can create one via permissionless mint() using a token they deploy that returns false on transferFrom when conditions aren't met.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL — Complete drain of userToken reserves from any pool where validatorToken returns false instead of reverting on transferFrom failure. Since pool creation is permissionless, an attacker could create pools with a malicious validatorToken designed to return false, then trick users into depositing or swapping userTokens into that pool, which can then be extracted. Alternatively, if any existing pool uses a legitimate token that happens to return false on failure (which some real tokens do), those pools are immediately vulnerable.
recommended_mitigations: Use OpenZeppelin SafeERC20 library: replace all IERC20.transfer() with SafeERC20.safeTransfer() and all IERC20.transferFrom() with SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom(). This handles both false-returning and non-returning tokens.
Reorder rebalanceSwap to follow checks-effects-interactions: perform IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom BEFORE updating state, or at minimum verify the transfer succeeded before proceeding.
Add a reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier) to all state-mutating functions to prevent cross-function reentrancy attacks that compound with this issue.
Consider validating actual balance changes (before/after pattern) instead of trusting transfer amounts, to also handle fee-on-transfer tokens.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC20 that returns false on transferFrom when allowance is insufficient (instead of reverting). 2. Deploy FeeAMM. 3. Create a pool using a standard ERC20 as userToken and the mock false-returning token as validatorToken. 4. Seed the pool with some userToken reserves (via executeFeeSwap from a helper account). 5. As the attacker, call rebalanceSwap with amountOut > 0 and zero allowance for validatorToken. 6. Verify that userTokens are received by the attacker without any validatorTokens being deducted from the attacker's balance. 7. Verify pool.reserveValidatorToken is inflated (phantom balance) while pool.reserveUserToken is drained.
uncertainties: The practical exploitability depends on whether the specific validatorToken in a pool returns false vs reverts on transferFrom failure. Most modern well-audited tokens revert, but some older or non-standard tokens return false.
Pool creation is permissionless, so an attacker could deploy a custom token that strategically returns false, but they would need victims to provide userToken liquidity to that pool.

The Solidity 0.8.28 ABI decoder behavior for discarded return values needs to be empirically verified, though this is well-documented behavior — bool returns are decoded but discarded when not captured.
additional_testing_needed: Empirical Foundry test: deploy mock false-returning ERC20, execute the full attack path, verify free token extraction
Survey of TIP-20 stablecoin implementations to determine which (if any) return false vs revert
Test with Solidity 0.8.28 specifically to confirm ABI decoder behavior when interface return value is discarded
Test the same vulnerability pattern in executeFeeSwap (where userToken.transferFrom return is also unchecked, and state updates + outgoing transfer follow)
